architecture_summary: The paper abstracts Transformers into a general architecture called MetaFormer, where the token mixer is not specified, and instantiates it with an embarrassingly simple pooling operator to build PoolFormer. PoolFormer is a hierarchical 4-stage vision model with patch embedding, Modified Layer Normalization, pooling-based token mixing, channel MLPs, residual connections, stochastic depth, and LayerScale.

novel_contributions: We abstract Transformers into a general architecture MetaFormer where the token mixer is not specified.
We deliberately replace attention with an embarrassingly simple pooling operator and build PoolFormer.
We show PoolFormer achieves competitive performance on ImageNet-1K, COCO, and ADE20K.
We argue that MetaFormer is actually what you need for vision instead of focusing on specific token mixers.

key_claims.claim: The paper proposes MetaFormer as a general architecture abstracted from Transformers by not specifying the token mixer, and argues that the architecture is more important than the specific token mixer.
PoolFormer replaces attention with pooling as a non-parametric token mixer and still achieves competitive performance on multiple computer vision tasks.
PoolFormer-M36 achieves 82.1% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K, surpassing DeiT-B/ResMLP-B24 by 0.3%/1.1% accuracy with 35%/52% fewer parameters and 50%/62% fewer MACs.
PoolFormer-S24 reaches more than 80 top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K with only 21M parameters and 3.4G MACs.
On COCO val2017, PoolFormer-based models consistently outperform comparable ResNet counterparts for object detection and instance segmentation.
On ADE20K validation set, PoolFormer-based models consistently outperform CNN-based ResNet and ResNeXt backbones as well as Transformer-based PVT.
Ablations show that even identity mapping or random token mixing can retain reasonable accuracy, supporting the claim that MetaFormer is the key factor.
The default ImageNet-1K training recipe uses 300 epochs, batch size 4096, AdamW, MixUp, CutMix, CutOut, RandAugment, stochastic depth, and LayerScale.
